1.synthesis:
The torat ha-asham (“law of the guilt offering”) at 7:1–10 provides the priestly rubric parallel to the asham legislation of chapters 5–6. The text distinguishes between the method of blood manipulation—blood is “thrown” (zeriqah) against the altar sides (v. 2) rather than smeared on the horns as in the sin offering—and the distribution of meat portions. Three categories of priestly portions emerge in rapid succession: the fat and specific organs burned on the altar as YHWH’s portion (vv. 3–5), the guilt-offering meat eaten by the male priests in the sanctuary (v. 6), the burnt-offering skin going to the officiating priest (v. 8), and the grain offering portions distributed by preparation method (vv. 9–10).

The accumulation of priestly perquisite details serves a theological function: it demonstrates that legitimate priestly income is precisely defined, not a matter of priestly discretion or appropriation. YHWH sets the portions; the priests receive what is given, nothing more. This transparency in the sacred economy models the principle that those who serve at the altar serve YHWH’s community, not their own enrichment.
1.historical_context: In ancient Israel, the Levitical priesthood had no tribal land inheritance; their livelihood derived from offerings brought to the sanctuary. The careful enumeration of which portions belonged to which priests—officiating versus collective—prevented both priestly poverty and priestly corruption. The zeriqah (throwing/dashing) method of blood application at the altar sides, rather than the netinah (applying/daubing) used for sin offerings, is significant in sacrifice classification: blood manipulation mode was one of the primary ways the ancient Israelite identified which category of sacrifice was being performed. The detailed attention to grain-offering distribution by cooking method (pan, griddle, deep-fryer) in v. 9 reflects the reality that multiple preparation techniques produced different textures and quantities of food, requiring explicit guidance on who received which.
1.literary_structure: This pericope mirrors the structure of the sin-offering torat at 6:24–30 but expands it with more detailed priestly-income legislation. Where the sin-offering section concluded with the burned/eaten binary (6:30), the guilt-offering section expands into a mini-catalog of priestly entitlements that anticipates the longer priestly-portion legislation of 7:28–36. The pericope closes at v. 10 and is followed immediately by the peace-offering torat at v. 11, the only one that explicitly distinguishes subtypes (toda, neder, nedavah). Together vv. 1–10 and 11–36 form the second half of the double torat unit that structures Leviticus 6–7.
1.theological_application: The precise specification of priestly income reflects a principle of transparency in religious economics: those who serve at the altar have a legitimate right to live from the altar (1 Cor 9:13), and that right should be clearly defined rather than left to negotiation or custom. The danger the text guards against is not priestly need but priestly accumulation through ambiguity: if portions are not specified, those with power to decide will decide in their own favor. The three-way division—YHWH’s portion burned, the priestly collective portion, the officiating priest’s individual portion—models a system where the most powerful actor receives a defined share, not an uncapped portion. This speaks to every system of religious leadership compensation: clarity protects both leader and congregation.
1.christology: The guilt offering’s blood-throwing (zeriqah) against the altar sides, distinct from the sin offering’s careful smearing on the horns, points to the variety of atonement gestures in the Levitical system. Hebrews 9:22 synthesizes these multiple modes: “under the law almost everything is purified with blood.” Christ’s blood is presented in the New Testament through multiple sacrificial registers—as the Passover lamb slaughtered (1 Cor 5:7), as the sin offering whose blood entered the sanctuary (Heb 9:12), and as the guilt offering that satisfies the debt of moral violation (Isa 53:10). The officiating priest’s share—the skin of the burnt offering—finds its NT echo in the concept that the minister who labors in the word is worthy of double honor (1 Tim 5:17), a principle Paul explicitly roots in this priestly-portion precedent.

11.synthesis:
The torat ha-shelamim (“law of the peace offering”) in 7:11–21 is the most socially complex of the offering laws, because the peace offering (shelamim) is the only sacrifice where the lay offerer eats from the same sacrificial animal as the priests and—symbolically—as YHWH. The legislation distinguishes three subtypes: the toda (thanksgiving) offering, whose meat must be eaten on the day of sacrifice with both unleavened and leavened bread accompanying; and the vow (neder) and freewill (nedavah) offerings, whose leftovers may be eaten on the second day but must be burned on the third (vv. 15–17).

The time limits are not arbitrary: meat in hot ancient Near Eastern conditions spoiled quickly, but the urgency of the toda one-day limit exceeds what food safety alone requires. It signals that thanksgiving must be shared with the community immediately—the toda feast must overflow into hospitality before the day ends. The pigul (“tainted”) concept at v. 18 and the karet (“cut off”) penalty for eating while impure (vv. 20–21) establish that the peace offering’s communal meal is not merely a social occasion but a covenantal act whose conditions must be met.
11.historical_context: The toda (thanksgiving) offering had a unique social function in Israel: it was accompanied by leavened bread (unusual in sanctuary contexts, cf. 2:11) along with unleavened wafers, creating the bread component of a communal feast. The large quantity of bread required meant that a single family could not consume it alone: the toda was inherently communal, requiring the offerer to invite others to share in the celebration of YHWH’s deliverance. The pigul (“abomination” or “tainted”) concept—eating peace-offering meat on the third day renders the entire offering invalid—reflects the Israelite concern that sacred meat eaten outside its ordained time window pollutes the entire sacrificial act retroactively. The karet (“cut off from the people”) penalty for eating while impure (vv. 20–21) signals that the boundary between the holy communion meal and the ordinary is treated with absolute seriousness.
11.literary_structure: The peace-offering torat at 7:11–21 is the longest of the five laws and uniquely subdivides by offering subtype and time limit. The structure moves from the most restrictive to the punitive: having defined what is permitted (toda, then neder/nedavah), the text turns to what renders the entire act invalid (pigul and impurity). This negative movement (vv. 18–21) closes the section and prepares for the fat/blood prohibition at v. 22. The karet penalty for eating peace-offering meat while impure is the same penalty used for more egregious violations, signaling that the boundary between the holy communion meal and the ordinary is treated with absolute seriousness.
11.theological_application: The toda offering’s one-day deadline teaches that thanksgiving is urgently communal—it cannot be a private sentiment nursed indefinitely but must be proclaimed and shared before the sun sets. The Psalms of thanksgiving (e.g., Ps 22:25; 116:17) reflect this structure: the rescued person gathers the congregation and declares what YHWH has done. For Christian communities, the Eucharist (eucharistia = Greek for “thanksgiving”) inherits this logic: it is inherently communal, set within specific time limits, and invalidated if eaten with unaddressed sin among the congregation (1 Cor 11:27–30). The pigul concept—eating outside the ordained time window invalidates the offering retroactively—warns against casual participation in sacred meals: timing and community are constitutive of the act.
11.christology: The peace offering is the sacrifice that most directly images the covenant meal shared between YHWH, the priests, and the offerer. Its three-party structure—YHWH’s portion burned on the altar, priests’ portion eaten in the sanctuary, offerer’s portion eaten within the household—anticipates the new covenant meal where Christ is simultaneously the sacrifice, the priest, and the host. John 6:51 (“the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my flesh”) draws on the peace offering’s bread-and-meat-together structure. The toda offering—thanksgiving for specific deliverance—has been identified by interpreters as the sacrifice that will outlast all others in the eschatological age: everything burns away except the thanksgiving that celebrates YHWH’s saving acts. Revelation 7:12’s heavenly chorus of thanksgiving before the throne of the Lamb is the ultimate toda feast.

22.synthesis:
This brief but weighty pericope interrupts the peace-offering legislation to establish two absolute prohibitions: fat from sacrificial species must not be eaten (vv. 23–25), and blood from any animal must never be consumed (vv. 26–27). Both prohibitions are grounded in divine ownership: the fat of ox, sheep, and goat belongs to YHWH and must be burned on the altar; blood belongs to YHWH because it is the nefesh (life-substance) of the creature. The fat of animals that die naturally (neveilah) or are torn by predators (terefah) is technically exempt from the altar-burning requirement—it may be put to non-food uses (v. 24)—but cannot be eaten under any circumstances.

The blood prohibition is universal: “in any of your dwelling places” (v. 26) and “any blood” removes every geographical and zoological qualification. Both violations carry the karet penalty—being cut off from the people (v. 25, v. 27)—placing them among the gravest covenant violations in the book.
22.historical_context: The fat prohibition was specific to the three main sacrificial species (ox, sheep, goat) and targeted the chelev—the hard suet surrounding the organs rather than the muscular fat marbled through the meat. This is an important distinction: Israelites ate meat normally, including its intramuscular fat; only the organ-surrounding fat portions were YHWH’s exclusive portion. The blood prohibition, by contrast, was universal and predated Sinai: Genesis 9:4 establishes it for all humanity after the flood, grounded in the equation blood = life. The Jerusalem Council in Acts 15:20, 29 retained this prohibition for Gentile believers alongside sexual immorality and food sacrificed to idols, suggesting that this Noachic command was understood as spanning the covenant communities.
22.literary_structure: This pericope functions as an interruption that becomes a clarification: the peace-offering legislation (vv. 11–21, 28–36) is bracketed by this central prohibition unit, which explains why certain portions of the sacrificial animal are non-negotiably excluded from human consumption. The bracketing structure (peace offering → fat/blood prohibition → peace offering portions for priests) teaches that every act of feasting at the altar occurs within the constraint of YHWH’s prior claim on life and vitality. The karet penalty for both violations places them among the most serious offences in Leviticus—alongside Yom Kippur desecration, Molech worship, and consulting mediums (20:6).
22.theological_application: The fat and blood prohibitions teach that even within legitimate feasting there are reserves that belong entirely to God and may not be touched. Not all of the animal is human food: the vital substances—the life-blood and the rich suet—are returned to their Owner. This principle models a broader pattern: human flourishing within covenant is always bounded by what belongs inescapably to the Creator. The blood prohibition’s Noachic grounding (Gen 9:4) suggests that this is a creation ordinance, not merely a Sinaitic ritual: the reverence for life-blood runs through the entire biblical narrative. Communities formed by this text approach the natural world—including the slaughter of animals for food—with a consciousness that life is sacred because it belongs to God.
22.christology: The blood prohibition reaches its ultimate christological turn precisely in the Eucharist: John 6:53–56 commands drinking Christ’s blood in language that was—and was intended to be—shocking against the Levitical background. “Drink his blood” reversed the deepest Sinaitic taboo: the nefesh of Christ is not withheld from human consumption but poured out for it. The New Covenant operates on the logic that the blood of the Lamb, far from being forbidden, is the very substance of life for those who receive it. Hebrews 9:14 clarifies the mechanism: “the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God.” The blood, once reserved for the altar as YHWH’s possession, is given by YHWH’s own Son to the covenant community as the ground of their new life.

28.synthesis:
The second half of the peace-offering legislation specifies how the offering is presented and which portions belong to the priests. The lay offerer brings the fat-and-breast with his own hands (v. 30), performing a wave gesture (tenupha) before YHWH—a symbolic presentation and return, giving the portion to God and receiving it back as priestly food. The breast becomes the portion of “Aaron and his sons” collectively (all male priests, v. 31), while the right thigh (shoq ha-yamin) goes specifically to the officiating priest as his elevated portion (terumah, vv. 32–33).

The double formula—wave offering (tenupha) for the breast, elevated offering (terumah) for the thigh—distinguishes communal priestly income from individual priestly compensation. The passage closes with a theological rationale: YHWH has “taken” these portions from Israel to give to the priests (v. 34), making the priestly meal a divine gift-in-trust, not merely professional pay. This pattern of divine taking-and-giving grounds priestly income in YHWH’s own mediating activity rather than mere institutional custom.
28.historical_context: The tenupha (wave offering) gesture has been debated: it may have involved a lateral swinging motion, an up-and-down motion, or a presentation-and-return gesture toward the altar. The Septuagint translates it aphairema (“thing lifted off”), suggesting an elevating motion. The rabbinic tradition described a complex ritual in which the priest placed his hands under the offerer’s hands, and both made the gesture together—priest and layperson cooperating in the offering act. The right thigh as an individual priestly perquisite is paralleled in several ancient Near Eastern temple systems, where the right side of the animal was a premium cut associated with honor and power. Its allocation to the officiating priest rewarded specific service rather than distributing it equally, creating a performance-linked compensation structure.
28.literary_structure: This pericope completes the chiasm of Leviticus 7: the peace-offering torat that began at v. 11 is now fully articulated in its three movements—lay offerer’s time limits (vv. 11–21), fat/blood prohibition (vv. 22–27), and priestly portions (vv. 28–36). The closing summary at vv. 35–36 (“this is the portion of Aaron and his sons from the food offerings of the LORD, set apart for them on the day he presented them to serve as priests”) ties back to the ordination narrative of chapter 8. The word mishchah (“anointing-portion”) at v. 35 links the priestly income legislation to the act of anointing that confers priestly status, suggesting that their right to eat is inseparable from their calling to serve.
28.theological_application: The theology of YHWH “taking” and “giving” priestly portions (v. 34) models a pattern of mediated provision: YHWH receives from the people and redistributes through designated channels. This prevents the priestly class from demanding support as its natural due while also preventing the congregation from withholding support as if it were optional charity. The wave gesture—the offerer presenting the breast to God, God receiving it, then returning it to the priests—enacts the theological claim that all provision is first God’s before it is the minister’s. Paul’s argument in 1 Corinthians 9:13–14 that “those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel” rests on exactly this Levitical logic: the priestly-portion system established that legitimate religious service entails legitimate material support.
28.christology: The wave offering’s gesture of presentation-and-return anticipates the logic of Christ’s self-offering. Christ presents himself to the Father (Heb 9:14: “offered himself through the eternal Spirit”), and the Father returns him—in resurrection—as the living bread for the world (John 6:51). The breast of the peace offering—the heart of the animal, the seat of courage and life in ancient thought—goes to the whole priestly community, not merely the officiating priest. This is the portion of all who belong to the royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9): communion in Christ’s love is shared by the whole body, not concentrated in a hierarchy. The thigh—the portion of power and movement—goes to the one who served: individual faithfulness in ministry receives individual recognition in the economy of God.

35.synthesis:
The closing summary of Leviticus 7 functions not as new legislation but as a compendium formula that reaches back over chapters 1–7 to declare their collective authority. The phrase zot ha-torah le- (“this is the law of the…”) appears here for all six offering types: burnt offering (olah), grain offering (minchah), sin offering (chatat), guilt offering (asham), ordination offering (millu’im), and peace offerings (shelamim) (v. 37). This is not a new regulation but a closing doxological summary, anchoring all six laws to a single revelatory moment: “the day he commanded the people of Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD in the wilderness of Sinai” (v. 38).

The Sinaitic setting is invoked not merely as historical information but as legitimating authority: these laws have the weight of the covenant moment behind them. The inclusion of millu’im (ordination offering) in the summary is particularly significant—it anticipates chapter 8 while also retroactively placing the ordination ceremony within the sacrifice-law corpus, signaling that the founding of the priesthood is not separable from the offering system it exists to maintain.
35.historical_context: Summary formulas of this kind—zot ha-torah (“this is the law”)—appear throughout the legal sections of the Pentateuch as editorial markers signaling the close of a legal unit and its attribution to divine command. Similar formulas occur at Numbers 5:29, 6:21, and 19:14. The inclusion of millu’im (ordination offering) in this summary anticipates chapter 8 (the ordination narrative) while also retroactively including the ordination ceremony within the sacrifice-law corpus of chapters 1–7. The Sinai attribution formula (“which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai”) carries the weight of Mosaic authority throughout the Pentateuch and signals the close of a major canonical unit.
35.literary_structure: The zot ha-torah formula at 7:37–38 provides a formal inclusion with the opening of chapter 1 and particularly with the phrase “the LORD called Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting” (1:1). The entire sacrifice-law corpus (chs. 1–7) is thus bracketed by divine speech from the sanctuary and divine command from Sinai. The ordination offering (millu’im) in the summary list is the only offering type not yet explained in chs. 1–7; its inclusion here signals that the explanation will follow in chapter 8 and that the offering-law corpus is not complete until the ordination narrative is read. This forward-pointing function gives the summary both a retrospective and prospective character.
35.theological_application: Summary formulas matter in Scripture because they define the canon within the canon of a given legal corpus: what is included in the zot ha-torah list is authoritative. The inclusion of the ordination offering alongside the five standard types teaches that institutional worship infrastructure—the ordination of those who mediate—is not secondary to the act of sacrifice but integral to it. Churches that invest heavily in worship forms but neglect the formation and commissioning of ministry leaders have misread this summary. The Sinaitic attribution (“which the LORD commanded Moses”) also teaches that the warrant for liturgical practice is not human tradition or ecclesiastical convenience but divine command: worship is ordered by revelation, not invented by custom.
35.christology: The zot ha-torah summary anticipates the christological summary in Hebrews 10:1–10, which surveys the entire Levitical offering system to show that it pointed forward to a single offering that accomplishes what the repeated sacrifices could not: “by the will of God we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all” (Heb 10:10). As the zot ha-torah formula in 7:37–38 closes the first cycle of offering laws and opens toward the ordination narrative of chapter 8, so the Hebrews summary closes the exposition of Levitical priesthood and opens toward the “new and living way” Christ has inaugurated through his flesh (Heb 10:20). The six offerings compressed into the Levitical summary are fulfilled in the one offering of the Son.
